specification.topic-statement: The scrollback byte-range system calculates the byte offsets within a stored text line that correspond to a requested span of terminal display rows.

scope.in-scope: Computing the start byte offset, end byte offset, and end-of-line flag for a given contiguous range of terminal rows within a single scrollback line, taking into account line wrapping and embedded newlines (segments).
scope.boundary:
This topic reads line text, byte length, and layout metadata (segment widths, physical row count, terminal width) from the scrollback buffer. It does not modify any stored data.
A line's physical row count is the sum of segment row counts. Each segment row count is the ceiling division of that segment's display width by the terminal width, with a minimum of one row. The display width is measured in terminal columns accounting for ANSI sequences (skipped) and wide Unicode characters (counted by their column width). A zero-width segment still occupies one row.

This topic delegates conversion from a display-column offset to a byte offset within the text to a shared byte-offset lookup operation. If that lookup fails, this topic treats the failure as a zero-byte offset.

field.name: start row offset
field.note: zero-based offset from the line's first display row

field.name: row count
field.note: number of display rows to include

field.name: start byte
field.note: byte index into the line's text where rendering begins

field.name: end byte
field.note: byte index into the line's text where rendering ends (exclusive)

field.name: is line end
field.note: true if the range extends to the final row of the line

behavior.computing-the-start-byte:
If the start row offset is zero, the start byte is zero — rendering begins at the first byte of the line's text.
Otherwise, the calculation locates the byte within the text that corresponds to the beginning of the requested row. The line's layout is consulted to determine how many display rows each segment occupies (each segment is a newline-delimited portion of the text). Each segment's row count is the ceiling of its display width divided by the terminal width, with a minimum of one.
Segments are consumed in order until enough rows have been accounted for. If the target row falls within a segment, the display columns advanced within that segment are computed (how many full terminal-width rows of that segment are skipped multiplied by the terminal width). A byte-offset lookup converts those display columns to a byte position within the segment's text.
If the target row crosses multiple segment boundaries, the byte position of the start is located by counting newline characters in the text starting from the byte found by the lookup. The position immediately after the Nth newline (where N is the number of additional segment boundaries crossed) is the start byte.
If the byte-offset lookup fails, the start byte is treated as zero.
behavior.computing-the-end-byte:
If the end row offset (start row offset plus row count minus one) is at or beyond the last display row of the line, the end byte is the total byte length of the line's text. The is-line-end flag is set to true.
Otherwise, the end byte is calculated using the same segment-walking logic as the start byte, but accumulating rows inclusively through the end row. The is-line-end flag is set to false.
If the byte-offset lookup fails during end-byte calculation, the end byte is treated as zero.
behavior.combined-range-calculation:
When a range is requested, the start and end bytes are computed separately and independently. The end row offset passed to the end-byte calculation is the sum of the start row offset and row count minus one.
All three output values (start byte, end byte, is-line-end flag) are produced by a single call that internally delegates to the start and end calculations.
behavior.notable: If the underlying byte-offset lookup reports an error (e.g., the display column is out of range), the affected start or end byte is silently set to zero rather than propagating the error. This may produce incorrect render ranges without any indication of failure.
